query: is sox10 expressed in melanoma cells

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Evaluation of Tumors -- Clinical Significance -- Malignant melanocytic neoplasms
retrieved_docs.content: Desmoplastic melanoma is a distinct variant that manifests as an intradermal, sarcomatoid spindle cell proliferation, commonly developing on severely sun-damaged skin. This neoplasm has a high mutation burden with a strong UV radiation signature and frequently harbors NF1 inactivation and other MAPK pathway-activating mutations. While it predominantly occurs in areas with severe elastosis, desmoplastic melanoma can also arise in acral or mucosal sites, sharing genomic characteristics with melanomas from those locations. [30] On IHC studies, the tumor is typically positive for S100 protein, SOX10, and nerve growth factor receptor (NGFR) but negative for HMB45 and melan-A. Additionally, PRAME expression has been observed in 35% of cases. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Evaluation of Tumors -- Issues of Concern -- Melanocytic tumors
retrieved_docs.content: Sex-determining region Y-related high-mobility group box 10 (SOX10) is a highly sensitive marker for both benign and malignant melanocytic lesions, including nearly all primary melanomas. However, its staining may be less intense in metastatic melanomas. SOX10 is particularly useful for distinguishing desmoplastic melanoma from histologically similar lesions, such as fibrohistiocytic and mesenchymal neoplasms. However, this marker should not be used alone for diagnosing metastatic lesions due to its expression in some breast and salivary gland carcinomas. [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Mohs Micrographic Surgery Management of Melanoma and Melanoma In Situ -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: MART-1, SRY (sex-determining region Y)-box 10 (SOX-10), and microphthalmia-associated transcription factor (MITF) are 3 commonly utilized immunohistochemical stains in MMS for melanoma. MMS with MART-1, a sensitive and specific cytoplasmic melanocyte stain, is effective for both MIS and invasive melanoma, with low local recurrence rates and equivalent or greater survival rates than WLE. [7] [21] SOX-10, a nuclear transcription factor, exhibits high sensitivity in various subtypes of melanoma, including desmoplastic melanoma, and can also be used in MMS. [13] Both SOX-10 and another nuclear melanocyte immunostain, MITF, offer a distinct outline of melanocyte nuclei and represent alternatives to MART-1 during MMS. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Evaluation of Tumors -- Clinical Significance -- Metastases to the Skin
retrieved_docs.content: Distinguishing cutaneous metastatic melanoma from primary dermal melanoma can be challenging. Metastatic melanoma cells are confined to the dermis, lack an overlying precursor lesion, and extend into the deep dermis. These cells often show little pigmentation and test positive for S100 or SOX10, though staining for melan-A and HMB-45 can vary. [77]

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Evaluation of Tumors -- Clinical Significance -- Malignant melanocytic neoplasms
retrieved_docs.content: Histologically, in situ ALM is characterized by an increased number of irregularly distributed basal melanocytes with hyperchromatic nuclei and prominent dendrites, progressing to confluence and eventual dermal invasion (see Image. In Situ Acral Lentiginous Melanoma). Acral melanomas generally have a low tumor mutation burden but frequently harbor oncogenic mutations in BRAF , NRAS , KIT , and NF1 , along with recurrent amplifications of CCND1, TERT, CDK4, GAB2, and MDM2 . [36] On IHC testing, SOX10 and MITF help assess intraepidermal melanocyte density and distribution in early lesions, while SOX10 and S100 are preferred for delineating melanocytes in desmoplastic acral melanoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Evaluation of Tumors -- Clinical Significance -- Mesenchymal (Soft Tissue) Neoplasms
retrieved_docs.content: MPNSTs typically arise from a benign nerve sheath tumor and are distinguished by spindle cells with marked cellular atypia. These cells are arranged in fascicles or whorls. Unlike benign neurofibromas, MPNSTs lack a myxoid background and Schwannian differentiation. The lesions exhibit high mitotic activity and show patchy or focal expression of S100 and SOX10.

retrieved_docs.title: Mohs Micrographic Surgery Section of Specimens Using Cryostat, Stain, and Immunostain -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Staining is performed after the specimen is cut on the microtome and transferred to a glass slide. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining is the most commonly used method in MMS. [1] Toluidine blue is used less frequently and is primarily applied when treating basal cell carcinomas (BCCs). [1] Immunohistochemistry is mainly utilized for melanomas, with the melanoma antigen recognized by T cells (MART-1) stain being the most common in MMS. Other immunohistochemistry stains for melanoma include SOX10, PRAME, HMB-45, MEL-5, and S100. [1] [5] [6] While immunostaining with cytokeratins can be helpful for detecting BCCs and squamous cell carcinomas (SCCs), few surgeons use this method due to the increased time and cost involved. [1] Reports have indicated that cytokeratin stains can be useful for confirming perineural invasion in SCCs and BCCs. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Neurofibroma -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Desmoplastic melanoma: An invasive melanoma that often resembles a dermal scar. Frequently there is an associated melanoma in-situ. Often large at diagnosis, there can be peripheral lymphoid aggregates and cytologic atypia. Immunohistochemistry: S100 and SOX10 (+). [33] [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Mohs Micrographic Surgery Management of Melanoma and Melanoma In Situ -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Microscopic examination: When treating melanoma, the debulking specimen and the tissue layer are examined under a microscope using H&E staining and applicable immunostains such as MART-1, SOX-10, or MITF. If any residual tumor is identified, then this area with the residual tumor is marked on the Mohs map and removed from the patient's skin. [1] [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Lentigo Maligna Melanoma -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: As lentigo maligna becomes more invasive (ie, progresses to LMM), junctional nests become larger and more spindled. Invasion is typically superficial, consisting of isolated or small aggregates of spindled cells in the papillary dermis. Desmoplasia and perineural invasion may occur, and the desmoplastic component may be mistaken for a scar. Rarely, a storiform growth pattern may be present, which may be mistaken for dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans. [2] [37] [38] Immunohistochemical staining using markers such as MART-1/Melan-A, HMB-45, tyrosinase, MITF, Sox10, and S100 supports accurate diagnosis. [2] [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Epidermis Histology -- Histochemistry and Cytochemistry
retrieved_docs.content: However, their use has limitations because of the variability in sensitivity and specificity. Therefore, in most cases, multiple stains must be used to narrow the diagnostic possibilities. For example, in the diagnosis of melanocytic tumors, some stains such as S100, SOX10, and Mart-1 stain are highly sensitive and are often used not only to confirm the lineage of the neoplasm but also to highlight its overall architecture.

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Evaluation of Tumors -- Clinical Significance -- Mesenchymal (Soft Tissue) Neoplasms
retrieved_docs.content: Schwannomas typically exhibit classic Antoni A or Antoni B areas. These tumors are composed of spindle-shaped cells arranged in distinctive patterns. Granular cell tumors consist of epithelioid cells with abundant granular eosinophilic cytoplasm. These cells stain diffusely positive for S100 and SOX10, aiding in their identification.

retrieved_docs.title: Cutaneous Squamous Cell Carcinoma -- Histopathology -- Desmoplastic
retrieved_docs.content: This subtype may appear similar to the sarcomatoid or spindle cell variant of squamous cell carcinoma. However, desmoplastic carcinoma is characterized by a desmoplastic (densely collagenous) stroma in more than 30% of the tumor. Perineural invasion is frequently reported. Desmoplastic melanoma is also considered in the histological differential diagnosis. Unlike desmoplastic melanoma, desmoplastic squamous cell carcinoma typically stains positive for p63 and negative for SOX10 and S100.

retrieved_docs.title: Melanoma of the Head and Neck -- Histopathology -- S100
retrieved_docs.content: A common marker for neural tissue. Acidic protein in the nucleus and cytoplasm

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Epidermis Histology -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Melanocytes, under normal conditions, are found intermittently studded in the basal layer. Melanocytes synthesize and store melanin in organelles known as melanosomes. Melanocytes transfer melanosomes via dendrites, primarily to keratinocytes. [4] The melanin content in the skin is responsible for variations in pigmentation. Altered distributions of melanocytes or destruction of melanocytes occur in various pathological conditions leading to hypopigmented areas of skin. Melanocytes are found in the basal layer at a ratio of roughly one melanocyte to 10 keratinocytes when evaluating a histological slide in two dimensions or a ratio of 1 melanocyte to roughly 40 keratinocytes in 3D space. Melanocytes have a variety of stains, but some of the most common ones are S100, Mart-1, MITF, and SOX10.

retrieved_docs.title: Mohs Micrographic Surgery Section of Specimens Using Cryostat, Stain, and Immunostain -- Technique or Treatment -- Immunostaining
retrieved_docs.content: Other melanocyte immunostains, such as SOX10, Mel-5, S-100, and HMB-45, are less commonly used. [13] Cytokeratin immunostaining may be utilized for SCC and BCC to improve tumor detection; however, the additional time and cost associated with this stain make H&E a more practical choice. [1] If cytokeratin staining is performed, the most practical option is AE1/AE3, which targets both high- and low-molecular-weight cytokeratins and can identify both SCC and BCC. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2565
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and are classified as a type of skin cancer. They are distinct from “سرطان الشفة الخبيث,” the Arabic term for malignant lip cancer, which represents a separate oncologic entity affecting the lip rather than the skin. Although one of the provided relationships incorrectly linked melanomas of skin to a finding site of “ret” (suggesting the retina), the correct anatomical location for cutaneous melanoma is the skin itself, not the retina. Consequently, melanomas of skin and malignant lip cancer differ in both their primary sites and their disease classifications, underscoring the importance of accurate anatomical and taxonomic identification in patient diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

Melanomas of skin are a distinct subtype of malignant skin cancer classified under “سرطان الجلد الخبيث”. Although an inverse‑ISA links melanomas of skin to “سرطان الشفة الخبيث”, this reflects their shared placement within the broader malignant epithelial neoplasm category rather than identity; melanomas arise in cutaneous tissue (primary finding site denoted by “ret”) and differ from lip cancer in etiology, behavior, and therapeutic approach. Together, these hierarchical, mapping, and inverse‑ISA relationships organize primary and metastatic neoplasms within comprehensive classification systems that support clinical coding, research, and patient‑centered management.
